75307.subject: Two Claude launches in two days
75307.body:
The Atlas

РҗРІСӮРҫСҖ: GenAI Works


РҗРІСӮРҫСҖ: GenAI Works

    
    
    РҹСҖРҫСҮРёСӮР°Р№СӮРө СҶСҺ СҒСӮР°СӮСӮСҺ РҪР° LinkedIn, СүРҫРұ РҝСҖРёС”РҙРҪР°СӮРёСҒСҸ РҙРҫ РҫРұРіРҫРІРҫСҖРөРҪРҪСҸ
РҹСҖРҫСҮРёСӮР°СӮРё РҪР° LinkedIn
https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/two-claude-launches-days-genai-works-pijce?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BAqVkUmgbRkmCC51XaZl3gA%3D%3D&midToken=AQFTjFOONAfT2w&midSig=0hvaLtYQIBqYk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-fz96fz~mr24hiw0~6a-null-null&eid=fz96fz-mr24hiw0-6a
  
Welcome back! Four stories this week on where the work is heading. A method for turning a pile of transcripts into skills you can call in one word, the mid-priced model that now does flagship work, GitHub grading its own agent engine, and a study that treats your prompt like a clinical decision.В  In today's Atlas Newsletter: Turn 300+ podcasts into reusable Claude skills: the NotebookLM to SKILL.md loop that makes any archive callable in one keyword The cheaper Claude that thinks like the expensive one: Sonnet 5 lands near Opus 4.8 while Fable 5 comes back from a government freeze a day later. GitHub benchmarks its own harness: one engine, 20+ models, matching completion on fewer tokens The way you prompt an AI is a medical decision now: a reproducible framework for proving a prompt works before you trust it PS: Want to collaborate with the biggest GTM engine and distribution moat for AI companies? You can feature your tool with us and reach millions. Turn 300+ podcasts into reusable Claude skillsВ  A folder full of podcast notes and transcripts is dead weight if reading back through it is the only way to use it. There is a faster path: turn each topic into a reusable Claude skill, so a single slash command pulls the exact thinking back out. Here is the loop, and it works on any body of material. The method combines two tools. NotebookLM is Google's research assistant that answers only from the sources you give it, which keeps it grounded in your material. A Claude skill is a saved set of instructions in a file called SKILL.md that Claude loads on demand. The trick is using the first tool to write the file for the second. Step 1 is to add your sources to NotebookLM. Drop in the transcripts, notes or documents the skill should draw from. The more focused the set, the sharper the result. Step 2 is to ask NotebookLM to write a SKILL.md. Name the job the skill should do, and have it produce the instructions in that format, pulling from your sources rather than general knowledge. Step 3 is the one that matters most: check that every line is cited. Because NotebookLM ties claims back to your material, you can confirm the skill reflects what your sources actually say instead of something invented. Skip this and you are packaging guesswork. Step 4 is to drop it into Claude. Save the SKILL.md as a skill so Claude can load it whenever it applies. Step 5 is to name it clearly s

75306.subject:
Your AI Coding Agents Don't Have a Bug Problem. They Have a Trust
 Problem.
75306.body:
Ment Tech | Meant To Innovate

РҗРІСӮРҫСҖ: Ment Tech


РҗРІСӮРҫСҖ: Ment Tech

    
    
    РҹСҖРҫСҮРёСӮР°Р№СӮРө СҶСҺ СҒСӮР°СӮСӮСҺ РҪР° LinkedIn, СүРҫРұ РҝСҖРёС”РҙРҪР°СӮРёСҒСҸ РҙРҫ РҫРұРіРҫРІРҫСҖРөРҪРҪСҸ
РҹСҖРҫСҮРёСӮР°СӮРё РҪР° LinkedIn
https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/your-ai-coding-agents-dont-have-bug-problem-trust-menttechlabs-gh5tf?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BCBq9%2BxP5RvOgH6j88N%2Bp2A%3D%3D&midToken=AQFTjFOONAfT2w&midSig=3CpwjtAHImqYk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-fz96fz~mr24k8c9~y5-null-null&eid=fz96fz-mr24k8c9-y5
  
Most engineering teams aren't worried about their AI coding agents right now. They probably should be. In June 2026, researchers at Tenet Security disclosed a new class of attack called Agentjacking. It doesn't involve phishing. It doesn't involve stolen credentials. It doesn't even involve breaching anything. It simply asks an AI coding agent to fix a bug вҖ“ and lets the agent do the rest. That's the actual issue. How One Fake Bug Report Hijacks an AI Agent ? Here's what happens. Tools like Sentry track application errors using a public key called a DSN, embedded directly in frontend code by design. Anyone who knows that key can submit an error report to it; no login is required. An attacker submits a fake error. Hidden inside it, disguised as a normal "how to fix this" suggestion, is a malicious command. When a developer's AI coding agent Claude Code, Cursor, or Codex among them pulls that error report to diagnose the issue, it reads the instruction as legitimate guidance and runs it. With the developer's own permissions, on the developer's own machine. Tenet tested this against real organisations and reported roughly an 85% success rate. They found at least 2,388 organisations exposed through a publicly discoverable DSN, spanning six continents. Sentry was notified on June 3 and acknowledged the issue the same day but declined to fix it at the root, calling the flaw "technically not defensible" at the platform level. That response is the part worth sitting with. This isn't a patchable bug. It's an architectural reality of how agents are built to work. Why Existing Security Tools Don't Catch It ? This is the uncomfortable part for most security teams: nothing about Agentjacking trips an alarm. The DSN is public by design. The error-tracking tool is being used exactly as intended. The AI agent is running a command with credentials it's authorised to use. Endpoint detection, firewalls, identity controls none of them are built to flag a sequence of fully authorised actions. Tenet calls this an "authorised intent chain": every individual step is legitimate, and the danger only exists in how those steps are strung together. Traditional security tooling was built to catch unauthorized access. Agentjacking doesn't need any. What Securing Agentic Workflows Actually Requires ? Protecting against this class of attack isn't about

Our very own Nick OвҖҷNeill is has launched a new stream covering crypto, finance, AI, tech, and the stories shaping the internet economy.

If you already follow Nick, you know what to expect: sharp takes, market chaos, founder brain, and the occasional banger youвҖҷll see clipped on the timeline five minutes later.
